findings.title: Bootstrap CI recomputes full resample over the entire historical trade set on every 5-minute bucket rollover, with no bound on the ledger's growth
findings.why_it_matters: bootstrap_mean_ci builds a numpy array of shape (10_000, n) via rng.integers(0, n, size=(resamples, n)) and again for arr[idx] — O(resamples * n) time AND memory, where n is the total number of resolved trades a variant has EVER accumulated. compute_variant_stats() (called once per configured/spawned variant, unconditionally) reads the variant's FULL history via realized_pnl_rows with no window, cap, or memoization, and fundable_at_checkpoint() does a second, nearly-as-large bootstrap on the checkpoint-truncated history. Both run synchronously inside engine/main.py's _on_bucket_rollover, which fires every ~5 minutes (BUCKET_SEC) for the lifetime of the process, and the ledger is explicitly designed to be append-only with no pruning ('the ledger grows unboundedly over the system's lifetime'). At n=10,000 resolved trades for one variant, the resample index array alone is 10,000*10,000*8 bytes = 800MB, doubled by the payoff array; at n=50,000 this is tens of GB and minutes of CPU, executed twice per variant, every 5 minutes, forever, on the same thread that must also keep polling the market every 2-5s. This is a genuine, unbounded resource-growth trajectory baked into the architecture, not a hypothetical: the system is designed to run 24/7 and spawn new variants (challengers) over its lifetime, each with independently growing trade histories.
findings.suggested_fix: Decouple report generation from the poll loop's synchronous rollover hook (run it in a background thread/process so a slow bootstrap can't stall market polling), and bound the bootstrap's per-call cost — e.g. cap the resampled sample to the most recent K resolved trades (documented as a deliberate methodology change, since the docstring currently pins full-history reproducibility), or replace the (resamples, n) matrix build with a chunked/streaming resample loop that keeps memory O(resamples) instead of O(resamples * n). Also consider memoizing per-variant stats keyed on trade count so unchanged variants skip recomputation on rollovers where nothing new resolved.

findings.title: open_trades() scans the entire trades table with no variant/mode filter, called every live-mode risk check to size remaining allocation
findings.why_it_matters: Ledger.open_trades() (engine/ledger.py:318-331) has no WHERE clause on variant_id or mode — it LEFT JOINs the entire trades table against resolutions and returns every open-or-unresolved trade across ALL variants and modes ever recorded. RiskManager._remaining_allocation() (engine/risk.py:249-259) calls it and then filters the fully-materialized Python list down to one variant/mode with a list comprehension. This runs on every RiskManager.check() call in live mode (i.e. every ~3s poll tick within a live variant's entry window, until an entry is recorded for that bucket — see engine/main.py POLL_SEC=3.0). Because the ledger is intentionally never pruned and the system is designed to run many concurrent shadow variants plus spawned challengers indefinitely, this query's row count (and the JOIN/filter cost) grows with the total lifetime trade count of the whole system, not just the one live variant being sized — a full-table-scan-per-tick pattern that gets more expensive the longer the engine has been running.
findings.suggested_fix: Add optional variant_id/mode parameters to open_trades() (or a new open_trades_for(variant_id, mode)) and push the filter into the SQL WHERE clause instead of filtering the fully-fetched Python list in _remaining_allocation. Pair with an index on trades(variant_id, mode, status) to make the filtered query index-only.

findings.title: Daily live-cap checks (live_realized_pnl_on_day / live_trade_count_on_day) full-scan the trades table on the per-tick live entry path
findings.why_it_matters: live_realized_pnl_on_day (ledger.py:360-364) and live_trade_count_on_day (ledger.py:366-373) filter on mode='live' plus a ts range, but the only index on trades is idx_trades_variant_mode (variant_id, mode) — useless for a mode-only/ts-range predicate since variant_id isn't bound. Both are called from RiskManager._check_daily_caps (risk.py:211-225), which runs on every live-mode risk check, i.e. every poll tick (~3s) within a live variant's entry window until that bucket's entry is recorded. Since resolutions/trades keep growing forever across every variant and mode (shadow volume in particular, given multiple perpetually-running shadow challengers), these two queries do a full scan over an ever-larger table on the hot live-trading path — the same growth trajectory the task description calls out for the ledger overall.
findings.suggested_fix: Add CREATE INDEX idx_trades_mode_ts ON trades (mode, ts) to the schema so the daily-cap queries can use an index range scan instead of a full table scan.

residual_risks: The bootstrap-CI growth problem is not confined to analytics/report.py: engine/evolution.py reuses the same unbounded-resample pattern (bootstrap_diff_ci over a variant's full split history in propose_challengers, and fundable_at_checkpoint/paired_comparison in review_promotions) on an hourly cadence (EVOLUTION_HOOK_SEC). As evolution spawns more challengers over the system's lifetime, the number of variants whose full history gets resampled every hour grows too, compounding the same root cause.

There is no visible monitoring/alerting on report-generation or rollover-hook duration, so a bootstrap call that has grown slow enough to delay the next poll tick (or exhaust memory) would surface only as missed ticks / OOM in production, not as a measurable, actionable signal ahead of time.
testing_gaps: No test exercises the ledger/report path at a realistic long-run trade volume (e.g. thousands to tens of thousands of resolved trades for a single variant) to catch the bootstrap's O(resamples * n) memory/time blow-up before it surfaces in production.

No test asserts that open_trades(), live_realized_pnl_on_day, or live_trade_count_on_day stay bounded (or use an index scan) as the trades table grows with unrelated variants/modes — the current tests likely exercise these against small, single-scenario ledgers only.
